item.title: Paris Street; Rainy Day
item.lesson: The Paris in this picture was brand new. Baron Haussmann had spent two decades demolishing the medieval city and driving through the broad boulevards and uniform apartment blocks that define Paris today — and Caillebotte, a wealthy young engineer-turned-painter, made that new city his subject. A fashionable couple advances under an umbrella; other umbrellas scatter across a cobbled intersection near the Gare Saint-Lazare; everything is gray daylight, damp stone, and strange, dreamlike calm. Unlike his Impressionist friends' shimmering brushwork, Caillebotte painted with photographic crispness and a wide-angle depth that feels uncannily modern — pedestrians cropped mid-stride, the lamppost splitting the canvas. He was also the movement's great patron, buying his friends' unsellable canvases; his bequest of that collection to France, grudgingly accepted, became the core of the Musée d'Orsay's Impressionist galleries.
item.lookFor: Notice that almost no one in this crowded intersection looks at anyone else — Caillebotte caught the particular solitude of the modern city street a full century before sociologists named it.
